Okay, now we're up to step 13, huge promise. What can you say truthfully and without blushing that will set up a believable opportunity for your prospect? This is not an early step. You need to get your research ducks lined up first. You don't want to rely on common sense. You don't want to take things at face value. You don't want to use flawed intuition to guess at what your big promise is. I'd say this is one of the main mistakes that rookie copywriters make. When they sit down they think, what is the big promise? What is the big thing behind this ad or this sales message that's going to be talked about, that's going to be the essence of the offer, that's going to make this something special in the prospect's day. Not just the shock of the hook and the bringing them into this, but actually the big promise. If you'll come with me, I will give you this or I will offer you this or I will help you change or fix or whatever this. This is the huge promise that we're talking about. You should let the big promise come to you as you research and map out your message. Don't sit down and think you're going to knock this out first. You need to play around with what's going on. You need to get the features and benefits down. You need to get the USP down. You got to think about your hook. You got to bring in the offer. You got to think about the path and adventure. You're about to send your prospect on before he becomes a customer and what happens to him after he becomes a customer. It will come to you. What is the big overarching promise of this thing? One good hint, of course, is that the meek shall not inherit the largest market share. Being mild and reticent about this is not the way to go about it. We talk about big promise because it's big. You want to be the adult in the room who can fix what's wrong, eliminate fears, fortify the sense of power and confidence among the powerless and the unconfident. This is very big. A lot of people lead their lives of quiet desperation. They've never been on the inside. They don't get the special opportunities. They're not first in line for anything. They don't win anything. They're never in the catbird seat. They may even, in fact, be kind of afraid of what's going on. Maybe they haven't set aside enough money for retirement. Maybe there are things going wrong with their job, their relationship, things going wrong with the house, with the economy. They're just afraid and they're feeling a little unconfident. If you can come and fix any of those things, if you can be that person that they can look to and say, okay, I'll go with you, where's this thing taking me? Let's find out what's going to happen next. I'm on board. The human mind seems hardwired to misinterpret reality in weird ways. This explains all the misfires on big promise and advertising that miss what prospects actually want. It sounds like it should be true, but it isn't. This happens in multi-million dollar launches and this happens with the entrepreneur down the street with the deli shop. They just don't get down into what is really happening, what people really want, what is really driving the sales that are bringing home the profits. The main thing is to not pay attention to what people say they want. Pay attention to what they actually buy or what they actually do. A lot of people when they're surveyed, they will say, yes, I read the Bible. It's my favorite book, but in actuality, they don't. They say it because they think that's what they're supposed to say. A lot of people will say things like, oh yeah, I would buy a product if you did this, this, and this, and then some marketers go off and do this, that, and the other thing, and they come back and the people don't actually buy it. The car world is one example. People kept saying they didn't want to negotiate and one of the big car manufacturers came up with a line where there was no negotiation. There was just the sicker price, that's what you paid, you're done and out. They didn't do very well. People said they wanted that, but they didn't actually respond to it when it became real. Your research that you do into what people actually buy, how they actually operate in the world as opposed to what they say they do or what you think they do or what you think they should do, any research you do into this is going to put you light years ahead of competitors who wing it. I would say the average marketer out there thinks he understands the culture. He understands civilization, he understands salesmanship, and especially he thinks he understands his market. He thinks, they want this. I know it. I just know it in my heart that they want this. They're often wrong for all the reasons we've been talking about. They're too close to the product. They've been in it too long. They're kind of jaded. They're a little bored with all of the ads and the various marketing they've done. They want to try something new and they want to pop off the page and deliver a big bang. They really don't know what they're talking about. Online, this is important, online once you have your prospects eyeballs on your site, you are equal to competitors 20 times your size. You want to use that moment to break through the distrust, the chaos, and the lies that are out there. If you're selling a small piece of software, but it competes with, say, one of the big giants in software, at that moment that your prospect is looking at your site, you are equal at that point. You are on equal footing with the big giant because you've got the eyeballs. You have the attention at that point of the prospect. You can start to especially break through what you realize is the large companies tend to, if not lying outright, they tend to spin things a lot and they tend to generate a bit of chaos out there. What you can do is be that person that breaks through all of this and delivers the big promise of yes, we're not the biggest in the world, but we're the one that will pick up the phone when you call. We're the one that will deliver value, come out and fix it if it breaks. We will be the ones who will help you manage it once it gets going. Professional trick here is to deconstruct how you last bought something from either a salesman or an ad. This is a very common thing and I really want people to go through this. Think of the last time that you bought something. What triggered your interest? What captured your trust? When did you decide to buy and what was presented that did the trick? There was a process that you went through inside with your brain, with your heart, with your gut. You thought about it. You kind of tested your will. If I buy it, am I going to be anxious? What am I going to tell everybody else? What if I make a mistake? What if this isn't the real deal? Do I trust this guy? At some point, you're just going to decide to buy. Be thinking about this all the time. You're going to be buying a lot of stuff in the next year, in the next two years for the rest of your life. Start thinking critically about what the process that you're going through. This will give you insight into what your prospects go through before they become a customer. One headline that worked very well in a number of markets was why almost everyone is wrong and how they, and this worked in the fitness market. This worked in the dieting market. This worked in various business markets. The best hooks aren't just true, but they ring true while challenging our notion of truth. This whole idea of why almost everyone is wrong, if you're in a position where you're not just unique, but you're actually bucking the trends, then your big promise is that, stay with me. I'm going to show you how everything you thought was happening isn't really what's going on, and here's a better, different, faster, cheaper way to do it. Let's move on that. This is a little thing about advertising. I call it magic versus reality. I came up with this chart a long time ago. I call it the marketing rebel appealometer. Basically, it's a very simple chart. This is the chart. You'll be able to see it very clear on your materials, where almost everyone responds and almost nobody responds. Down here, nobody's responding. Up here, almost everyone is responding. On the cross part of this, it's cold hard reality, and over here is magic. Say you have a diet market. In the diet market, you want to talk about the absolute truth of how to lose weight being, you've got to stop eating so much, and you've got to exercise. You have to join a gym, and you have to not eat your favorite foods anymore. That's the cold hard reality. Almost nobody responds to that. However, if you invented a pill that I could take, and overnight I would lose all the weight I wanted to lose, and wake up skinny and would remain that way the rest of my life, if that was true, almost everyone would respond to that. That's kind of magic. Now, you'd also go to jail probably, because there are no pills that do that. However, somewhere in the middle here is where the cold hard reality meets the idea of magic. You say, look, basic way to lose a lot of weight very quickly and safely is to control what you eat, to get some kind of exercise going on, to get into this. However, that's very difficult for most people. Here is a program I have that makes it very easy. It might involve dance, it might involve some really good food, or maybe alternating meals. There's all kinds of ways you can do that to get towards that magic thing. Another example is, let's stick in the golf market. The cold hard reality of being able to be a better golfer is you've got to practice a lot. You have to get in better shape. You've got to spend all your spare time learning about golf and going out to the range and hitting endless balls and paying attention to a whole lot of stuff. That cold hard reality doesn't really wash very well with the market. You're not going to sell a lot. Almost nobody really wants to hear that, because we kind of know it. However, if you had, again, a pill that I could take where suddenly I would have a windmill swing, I'd be hitting 300-yard drives consistently straight and true down the fairway, and my putting would have some kind of alien craft hovering that no one could see that would guide the ball in, no matter where I was, so I always one-putted. That would be the magic that almost everybody would want to get involved with. But that's not true. That doesn't happen, and we know that doesn't. Somewhere in the middle is that mix where let me give you how people do get better, and let me give you an opportunity to get better. However, it's very easy. It's very simple. You don't have to practice a lot. You don't have to go out onto the practice range and spend half your life there. So you kind of meet in the middle, and that's where the appeal-a-meter makes it. Now, a lot of people like to invent ideas to get into the magic part of this, and that's just a mistake. You'll be caught sooner or later because there is no magic in the world, really. There is cold-hearted reality, and then there is things that defy the cold-hearted reality that a lot of people would want to force upon you. There are ways to make it a little more tolerable. There are ways to shortcut things. There are ways to get better results faster, cheaper, quicker, and more simply. So the sweet spot is to deliver the shocking truth that may be difficult to digest if introduced without context in a way that doesn't freak your prospect out. So in the golf example, it's like, yes, you can golf a lot better. You can do it really quick, and you can do it without a lot of practice. And that may be difficult to understand until in context you say, because we've done this already with thousands of golfers, because this is something that's been a closely held secret, even professional golfers for a long time, and now it's leaking out, and we're going to show it to you. So you explain what's going on. That's that sweet spot. So here's some headlines. This is an example I did for a chiropractic coach. This was a guy who taught chiropractors how to have a better life and a better business. And we looked into what was bothering chiropractors, and for a lot of doctors, the first thing they want to do after they spend their seven to ten years learning their craft, and they finally graduate, and they go out and they're hanging their shingle, and they see their first patient, and then the next thing they want to do is retire, because it gets hard, and they start losing sleep, they start having to manage the office. A lot of things happen, so that by the time they've been in business for a short period of time, they're behind. They're overworked, they're burned out, they're not making much money, and they don't really know what they're doing because they don't teach marketing in medical teaching colleges. So this headline was basically explaining this coach could come in and very quickly do some things for you. So the headline was, Who else wants to learn the secrets of earning an extra $96,485 as a chiropractor this year? While working just 24 hours a week or less with minimum stress and zero debt. This hits on all the magic points that most of the chiropractors have responded to this. If you could give them a magic wand where they would just wave it, and all their debt would go away because a lot of them were in debt from school, from getting their offices set up for various reasons, the stress was through the roof, and they were working 50, 60, 70 hours a week and more. And the idea of making a little bit more money was just a dream they never thought they'd attain. And this guy had simple ways where within a year all of this could happen. You'd start taking vacations with 24 hours a week, that becomes kind of a magic solution. It's like, if I could make more money, work less, have zero debt, and actually bring my stress level down, it would change my life. That was the kind of magic. In this ad, I actually had a notary public sign a little piece of paper that we put in the ad, that's saying that, yes, the average client of this guy made an extra $96,000. This was a true fact. We weren't pulling this out of our ass. It was something he could actually say, stand behind. And the 24 hours, he had charted over a long time. He knew how much these guys were working, he knew how he could get it down. In fact, he did by forcing them to take vacations every month and to take off a day a week and get down their hours. And of course, the stress naturally started to leave, and the debt thing was a whole other restructuring of debt and things like this. So he brought in some financial expertise, and he brought in some life management skills and all of this stuff, essentially producing magic because none of this was not only available to the average car manufacturer he was approaching, it wasn't even a pipe dream. They didn't even believe it was possible. And then this was an ad that I wrote a long time ago when I first started teaching freelancers, and it was how to turn your ability to string two sentences together into a freelance copywriting career, that if you'll just follow the simple map I've laid out for you, can earn you over $100,000 your first year. And that was the results that we were getting. I was taking freelancers and teaching them how to get clients, get better clients, to get more for each job, to be able to get royalties, to change the way they approached clients, to change the way they went from just being the guy who supplied copy to being the guy that came in and actually was part consultant. So we were able to do this really easily. The idea of how to take your ability to string two sentences together was that a lot of the writers I took, hadn't written anything, and they weren't even, not only were not writers, they weren't good at any part of writing. So we taught them how to start to write, what it took to be a good salesman, to infuse copy with salesmanship, all the stuff you're learning here, and we laid it out and we were able to back it up. So that's the big promise and that's something that you're going to need at some point in your ad where it just becomes the thing that the prospect can focus on when they're heading towards becoming part of your world. Okay, huge promise. I'm going to start off with, it's kind of an outline question, but I know at least I kind of know how you answer this question when I think it's informative and you didn't cover it in the lecture. The appealometer concept applies to almost every market, almost every person all the time. We've had other people come forward, but wait a minute, in my market, my people are different. Is that ever the case? Is that a global rule or maybe another way to think about that in different ways? Yeah, in fact I worked with the bodybuilding market for multiple years and we wrote ads where the hardcore reality of being able to pack on muscle and weight means you've got to become a gym rat, spend your life there lifting lots of weights, you've got to sweat a lot, you've got to devote your life to your diet and you have to hurt a lot because you're breaking down muscle, building a backup, you've got to do recovery, it's a pretty grueling thing and that actually appeals very well to guys in the market. They understand that, they know it and the more pain you can offer, the better the product will sell sometimes. But again, to clarify, you're not talking fitness, you're talking to people that are trying to make bodybuilders, they're trying to become massive, so when that perhaps one of the only outliers. Okay, the other questions and conversations that have come up over the years on this is, they all sit around the idea of what I'll just call believability. What if your promise is, you can fulfill it but you just think no one's going to believe this, I really am doing something here, I've stumbled onto something, is there ever, what strategies do you recommend to someone who has that? My promise might be too big. Yeah, that gets back to the one on one conversation. If you have a chance to talk to somebody and you actually say, this is my promise, I can do this, what is the reaction? Do they raise their eyebrows? Do they just look at you, stand up and walk away? Or do they say, come on, and how do you respond to that? Because you're going to bring up objections. So how do you respond to that? One of the classic ways that we do that in copy is we say, I know this sounds unbelievable, and I wouldn't believe it myself if I hadn't seen it with my own two eyes, but this really happens. Now, I don't expect you to believe it just because I say it's true, so let me go about proving it to you. Okay, now, so that's one case, right? Defend the big promise. What if the opposite is true? I'll just take as an example, the odds are everyone in the simple writing system who's either bought the course in the coaching program has seen another ad about marketing. This is probably something about internet making money, business opportunity. You've probably seen many ads that make outrageously crazy promises, like do nothing, click on this link, make a million dollars next month. In your underwear. Yeah, right, there you go, from your kitchen table in your underwear. We're in a market, as are others, weight loss finance, where there's many people making outrageous claims that let's just say are probably not true. What if you feel you're in a market like that where you can, like you know you're giving people 7% to 9% improvement. It's fallible, they can do this thing, you have success stories, testimonials, but people are used to seeing people saying, I'll give you double digit improvements or I'll make you millions when you know you can really help people make thousands. How do you help people, how do those people position themselves? You know, it's interesting, but the believability factor, you know, regardless of how true what you're saying is, the believability factor has to go through a little gauntlet in the prospect's mind of do I believe it as it's true as it's outrageous. And for example, in a number of markets, if you promise you'll make a million dollars with this, you won't get much response. If you say you know you're not going to make a million dollars, but I can guarantee that you'll make another $40,000 next year without putting any more work into it. You know, it's like you don't have to take on a part-time job. And that can resonate with a lot of people for whom $40,000 is a lot of money. It's a different part of the market. They're going to have to people who want to make a million dollars. You make it real. You say look, are you just confronted? A lot of people are making claims that they can't back up. They're going to make you claim I can back up. In six months, you're going to get these results. Or I'm going to give you all your money back. Not only will that, you know, I'll give you double your money back and whatever. You may have to go to extremes to be able to take away all the sense of risk or to be able to make them feel like, well, not only does this sound doable, but it really sounds like he's putting everything behind it. He's taking his reputation on it, actually putting his money where his mouth is. And there's a lot of reasons for me to think, okay, we'll test this. And you can even say later on, if you want to make more, you can probably make more too. But let's be real. You have a busy life. How about if we just talk about making an extra $40,000 in a couple of months? Would that prove it to you? Then we'll go on from there. Yeah, or even how about if you could make the car payment? Like I said, you can bring that down to let people find their level of believability in any case. You've used that before and that's a very good way to make it real. It's not so much the money that's going to come in and say, how would you like to not have to pay your car payment for the next six months? Well, we can make that happen. You'll still pay it. It's just the money will magically appear, or not magically, the money will appear from you putting in two hours a week. And that is something they can get in their head and they can think, they can actually respond to the doubters in their lives. They can say, honey, I'm going to use this for the car payment. I'm not going to use this to go off on a trip to Vegas without you. We're going to pay off the house faster with this. This is going to a specific thing. Okay. As part of the answer to the question, you started throwing some tidbits out for an upcoming section on reverse. Yes. I think what we'll call it here, again, another exercise to go with this that you'll explain, same model if you're with the program by now, if you're in the coaching program, you know how to post it. If you're just going through the materials on your own, hopefully maybe you're seeing what could be the high value of working with a professional copywriter, getting some feedback as you're going through this. If you're interested in joining the coaching program, that is something you can always add on now or sometime in the future. So there's somewhere on this page. Are you watching this video? There's more information about that. But as I said, that is something, if you're seeing the value, you kind of wish you would have done that. Let us know and we'll see if we can help you out. Okay. We've gone through the step and because we've gone through the 12 previous steps, you are now armed with the, a lot of the information where you can start to map out a huge promise. So why don't you write one down just right now with what you have, think about it and what can you say truthfully and without blushing that will set up a believable opportunity. What is the big overriding promise that you can make? Give it a shot and I think you'll, even if you misfire on the first one, I think you're going to learn very quickly through experience by doing this, that it becomes easier and easier. The more research you put into this, the more you've mapped out your USP, all of your bullets, your features and benefits, all of the things that we did up to now will help you get this down and get it down pat. Thank you.
